title: Research Program for Literacy Without Sound
subtitle: What could be studied next, why Brazil matters, and what support would make it possible
summary: A practical brief that turns the science framing into a buildable SignWriting research program without turning the project into a comparison contest.
synopsis: The next useful move is not more abstract defense of signed writing and not a head-to-head script competition. It is a concrete SignWriting-centered research program. This deck defines the main study lanes, the most realistic early studies, the role of Brazil as the strongest current empirical center, the data and support needed to carry the work, and what a host institution would make possible.

slides.title: From Framing to Program
slides.summary: The science framing is strongest when it leads to concrete studies rather than stopping at conceptual justification.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for research program transition.
bullets: Keep the science series as the canon
Build a practical study program around SignWriting
slides.speech: The science framing already does an important job. It establishes that language does not require sound, that literacy is a durable symbolic expansion, and that plane-based writing deserves serious attention. The next task is different. It is to define what gets studied next, where the strongest evidence base already exists, and what data, tools, and support are required to turn SignWriting from a compelling theory into a scalable research program.
slides.notes: Open by clarifying that this document follows the science series rather than replacing it.
slides.mermaid:
graph LR
  A[Science Framing] --> B[Research Program]
  B --> C[Studies]
  B --> D[Infrastructure]
  B --> E[Evidence]

slides.title: What This Program Is Not
slides.summary: The goal is not to fund a comparison contest, but to build evidence for SignWriting as a real literacy and research pathway.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for what the program is not.
slides.speech: A more concrete scientific program does not need to become a head-to-head script contest. It also does not need to drift into endless philosophical defense or overpromise expensive neuroscience too early. The stronger path is narrower and more constructive: build evidence for SignWriting as a readable, writable, durable symbolic pathway for signed language, and do so in ways that can grow into larger literacy, corpus, and cognition programs over time.
slides.notes: Keep the lane disciplined and constructive.

slides.title: Three Research Lanes
slides.summary: The most useful structure is a three-lane program connecting literacy, Brazil, and shared data-and-tool work.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for three research lanes.
slides.speech: The cleanest structure is a three-lane program. First, a reading and literacy lane focused on SignWriting as text. Second, a Brazil lane grounded in the strongest currently documented real-world ecosystem. Third, a data-and-tool lane that builds the corpora, tasks, and reusable materials needed to make the science cumulative rather than one-off.
slides.notes: This is the organizing slide. Keep it simple.
slides.mermaid:
graph TD
  A[Research Program] --> B[Reading and Literacy]
  A --> C[Brazil Empirical Center]
  A --> D[Infrastructure and Datasets]
  B --> E[Evidence]
  C --> E
  D --> E

slides.title: Reading and Literacy Lane
slides.summary: The first lane asks what happens when signed language gains durable readable and writable text.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for the reading and literacy lane.
bullets: Text use by signers with early sign-language access
slides.speech: The literacy lane focuses on what written SignWriting lets signers do as readers and writers. That includes reading comfort, comprehension, recall, annotation, revision, and routine use of text. The question is not only whether signs can be written. It is whether durable signed text changes literacy practice in ways that can be observed, measured, and built on.
slides.notes: Tie back to literacy without sound, not just to script form.

slides.title: Brazil as the Empirical Lane
slides.summary: Brazil provides the strongest currently documented base for moving from conceptual claims to real populations, settings, and materials.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for Brazil as the empirical lane.
bullets: Real written materials and teaching history
Best current base for early partnerships and studies
slides.speech: Brazil matters because it gives this program a realistic empirical center. The public record already shows institutional density, repeated teaching practice, and a deeper literature than most English-language conversations recognize. That makes Brazil the strongest current place to begin partnership, institutional mapping, material collection, and early literacy studies.
slides.notes: Reference the Brazil brief as the companion evidence base.

slides.title: Infrastructure and Dataset Lane
slides.summary: The science becomes much stronger when texts, corpora, tasks, and tools are built as shared resources rather than one-off project artifacts.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for infrastructure and dataset lane.
bullets: Reusable reading stimuli and task design
Shared tools for annotation, search, and analysis
slides.speech: The data-and-tool lane is what prevents the science from remaining fragmented. If texts, corpora, tasks, and software are built as shared resources, later studies become easier to replicate, compare, and extend. Without that lane, every study risks becoming an isolated artifact. With it, the program begins to accumulate.

slides.title: Realistic Early Studies
slides.summary: The first phase can be modest, practical, and still scientifically valuable.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for realistic early studies.
slides.speech: The early program does not need to start with expensive imaging or giant longitudinal claims. It can begin with an updated Brazil accounting, a materials audit, reader-experience studies, short SignWriting passage tasks, and corpus-building pilots. Those are realistic, affordable, and still strong enough to change the research landscape if done well.

slides.title: Who and What Get Studied
slides.summary: The strongest early populations and materials are already visible if the program stays focused.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for populations and materials.
bullets: Deaf signers with early sign-language access
Classroom materials, books, dictionaries, and short texts
slides.speech: A focused program already has viable populations and materials. Deaf signers with early sign-language access are central. Existing SignWriting learners and teachers are central. Classroom materials, books, dictionaries, and short texts are central. This is another reason Brazil matters: it helps turn these from hypothetical study components into reachable ones.
slides.notes: Keep the populations realistic and ethically legible.

slides.title: What Success Looks Like in Phase One
slides.summary: Phase one succeeds when it produces reusable evidence, not just isolated findings.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for what phase one success looks like.
bullets: Pilot studies that others can build on
slides.speech: Success in the first phase is not a grand claim about all signed-language literacy. It is more concrete. Success means an updated institutional map, curated materials and datasets, pilot studies other researchers can build on, and sharper grant-ready questions for later work in literacy, reading, and cognition.
slides.notes: Define success in a modest but meaningful way.

slides.title: What a Host Institution Enables
slides.summary: A host institution would let the research lane and the data-support lane reinforce each other instead of developing separately.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for host institution role in the research program.
bullets: Grant framing across literacy, linguistics, CS, and neuroscience
slides.speech: A host institution matters because it connects the study lanes to actual capacity. Partnership building, ethics-ready study design, data stewardship, documentation, and grant framing all become easier when there is a credible academic home. This is where the research program and the support work around it converge.
slides.notes: Tie back to Iowa/infrastructure lane without making it Iowa-specific.

slides.title: Bottom Line
slides.summary: The most useful next move is a SignWriting-centered research program that is empirical, support-aware, and modest enough to begin now.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for research program bottom line.
bullets: Use Brazil as the strongest current base
Let shared data and tools make the science cumulative
slides.speech: The bottom line is simple. A more concrete scientific program is a good idea if it stays SignWriting-centered, uses Brazil as the strongest current empirical base, and treats data, tools, and support as part of the science rather than as an afterthought. That is specific enough to begin now and strong enough to grow later.

graph LR
  A[SignWriting-Centered Program] --> B[Brazil Empirical Base]
  A --> C[Infrastructure]
